result:
The rain arrived without ceremony, just a slow hiss against the window that turned the city lights into watercolor bleeds. I sat at the small wooden desk I’d rescued from a curb three years ago, listening to the radiator clank like an old man clearing his throat. Outside, Seoul was swallowing itself in neon and steam. Inside, the only light came from the laptop screen and the ridiculous orange salt lamp I bought during a phase.

I’ve been thinking about how memory doesn’t sit still. It moves like those plastic snow globes children shake—except the snow never quite settles. Every time you look, the flakes are in slightly different places. Some have melted. Some have turned into entirely different colors.

Last week I found a voice memo on my phone from 2019. I don’t remember recording it. In the memo I sound half-drunk and completely certain that “the problem with people is they keep trying to become the person they would have been if they hadn’t been hurt.” I laughed when I heard it, because it’s both pretentious and embarrassingly accurate. Then I cried a little, because I realized I’ve spent the last five years trying to outrun that version of myself instead of sitting down with her.

There’s this Japanese concept called *wabi-sabi* that I keep coming back to like a tongue worrying a loose tooth. The beauty of imperfection. The idea that a cracked tea bowl is more valuable because it shows its history. I wonder what my cracked parts would look like if they were ceramic. Would they be gold-kintsugi beautiful or just ugly fractures that make the whole thing useless?

My neighbor plays the piano at 2:17 a.m. every Thursday. Not well. The notes stumble like someone learning to walk again after a long illness. I used to be annoyed by it. Now I set an alarm just to listen. There’s something about the way he attacks the same difficult passage over and over, failing differently each time. It feels like prayer.

I’ve been trying to write a novel for seven years. The working title changes monthly. Currently it’s “All the Houses We Burned Down While Looking for Light.” The main character is a woman who collects other people’s abandoned New Year’s resolutions. She has a storage unit full of them—boxes labeled “Learn Italian,” “Stop Apologizing,” “Actually Use That Expensive Camera.” She visits the unit every Sunday like it’s church.

Sometimes I worry I’m becoming her.

The thing no one tells you about getting older is that your ghosts start introducing themselves properly. They stop hiding in closets and start sitting across from you at breakfast. My ghosts have names now. Regret. The One Who Got Away. The Version of Me That Could Have Been a Scientist. They’re not even mean most days. They just want to be acknowledged. Like cats that knock things off shelves until you look at them.

I made soup tonight. Not because I particularly wanted soup but because the ritual of chopping onions and crushing garlic feels like holding the present moment in my hands. The knife going *thwack thwack* against the cutting board is proof that time is still moving forward, even when it feels like everything is looping.

My mother called while the soup was simmering. She asked if I was eating enough protein. I told her about the chicken I’d put in the soup. She told me about her new book club that’s reading something called “The Courage to Be Disliked.” We both pretended we weren’t worried about each other. The pretending is a kind of love language at this point.

There’s a spider living in the corner behind my toilet. I’ve named her Barbara. She’s built this ridiculous asymmetrical web that looks like it was designed by someone having a very confident anxiety attack. Every morning I check to see if she’s still there. Every morning I feel something close to relief when I see her. We’re both just trying to make something with what we’ve got.

I’ve been experimenting with talking to plants. Not in the cute houseplant-owner way, but out loud, complete conversations. I told my monstera about my fear that I’m emotionally unavailable because I’ve read too many books. She didn’t respond, but one of her leaves unfurled dramatically the next day, which I’m choosing to interpret as solidarity.

The city outside my window is never quiet. Even at 3 a.m. there are delivery scooters and drunk people singing trot songs and the occasional ambulance reminding everyone that time is serious business. I like that about Seoul. It refuses to pretend that life is tidy. It throws its mess right in your face and dares you to find beauty in it anyway.

Sometimes I stand on my tiny balcony and look at all the lit windows in the apartment building across the street. Each rectangle of light is someone’s entire universe. Someone is falling in love in one of them. Someone is probably giving up in another. Someone is undoubtedly eating ramyeon in their underwear while crying. We’re all just specks of light trying not to go out.

I deleted most of my dating apps this morning. Not because I’m giving up on love but because I’m exhausted by the idea that compatibility can be reduced to whether someone enjoys hiking and has a “healthy relationship with their mother.” I want to meet someone the old way—through a series of increasingly specific coincidences that feel like the universe showing off.

The soup is gone now. Just a faint garlic ghost in the kitchen.

I keep thinking about this line from a Mary Oliver poem: “Tell me, what is it you plan to do with your one wild and precious life?” I used to treat it like an accusation. Now it feels more like a gentle elbow to the ribs. Like the universe is saying, *hey, I see you over there, still folding your laundry like it’s a personality trait. Maybe try something else.*

So I’m trying something else.

I’m writing this at 4:12 a.m. while my neighbor’s off-key piano drifts through the walls like smoke. I’m thirty-six years old and I still don’t know what I want to be when I grow up. I own three pairs of scissors because I keep losing them and then finding them again in places I swear I looked. My houseplants are somehow still alive. I have thirty-seven tabs open that I will never read. I’m terrified and hopeful in equal measure, which seems about right.

The rain has stopped. The city is taking a breath.

In the silence between piano notes, I can hear my own heartbeat doing its stubborn, ridiculous work. Keeping me here. Keeping me in the story.

And for tonight, that feels like enough.

(Word count: 1002)

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
